title: Identifying Automatic Thoughts in CBT
clinical_goal: Help clients recognize and label automatic negative thoughts as the first step toward cognitive restructuring
content: Automatic thoughts are spontaneous, often fleeting cognitions that arise in response to situations. In CBT, clients are taught to notice these thoughts, write them down, and evaluate their accuracy. A thought record is the primary tool: the client records the triggering situation, the emotion felt and its intensity (0–100%), the automatic thought, and supporting or contradicting evidence. Common cognitive distortions to identify include all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophising, mind-reading, fortune-telling, and personalisation. The therapist uses Socratic questioning—'What is the evidence for and against this thought?'—to guide the client toward a more balanced perspective. With practice, clients can complete thought records independently between sessions.
short_summary: Teaches clients to notice, record, and examine automatic negative thoughts using thought records and Socratic questioning.
safety_notes: Reviewing distorted thoughts can temporarily increase distress; normalise this and build coping strategies alongside thought work.
contraindications: Not suitable as a standalone technique during acute psychosis or severe dissociation.

title: Behavioural Activation for Depression
clinical_goal: Increase engagement in rewarding activities to break the withdrawal–depression cycle
content: Behavioural activation (BA) is a first-line, evidence-based treatment for depression. Its core principle is that depression is maintained by avoidance and withdrawal, which reduces access to positive reinforcement. The intervention proceeds in four steps: (1) psychoeducation—explain the link between activity, mood, and reinforcement; (2) activity monitoring—the client tracks daily activities and mood ratings for one week using an activity diary; (3) activity scheduling—collaboratively identify and schedule activities that are pleasurable (e.g., hobbies), meaningful (e.g., values-aligned tasks), or necessary (e.g., self-care); (4) graded task assignment—break overwhelming tasks into small, achievable steps to build momentum. The therapist reviews the diary each session, reinforces completed activities, and troubleshoots barriers. BA is equally effective to cognitive therapy for moderate-to-severe depression.
short_summary: Uses activity monitoring and scheduling to reverse the avoidance cycle driving depression, restoring access to positive reinforcement.
source_name: Martell, Dimidjian & Herman-Dunn — Behavioral Activation for Depression
safety_notes: Watch for over-scheduling that creates pressure; ensure activities are realistic given the client's current energy.
contraindications: Requires motivation to track activities; not suitable during acute suicidal crisis without appropriate safety planning.

title: The ABC Model of CBT
clinical_goal: Teach the relationship between activating events, beliefs, and emotional/behavioural consequences to promote cognitive change
content: The ABC model, originating from Albert Ellis's Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy and widely adopted in CBT, provides a framework for understanding how thoughts mediate the relationship between events and emotions. A = Activating event (the situation or trigger); B = Beliefs (automatic thoughts, assumptions, or core beliefs); C = Consequences (emotions and behaviours). A key insight is that A does not directly cause C; instead, B (what we tell ourselves about A) determines C. Clinically, therapists use the ABC model to help clients identify the belief layer they may overlook when attributing distress solely to external events. The model can be extended to ABCDE: D = Disputing irrational beliefs and E = new Effect (a healthier emotional and behavioural outcome). Clients practise completing ABC worksheets between sessions.
short_summary: Explains that emotions and behaviours (C) are driven by beliefs (B) about events (A), not by events alone—foundational to all CBT work.
source_name: Ellis & Harper — A Guide to Rational Living

title: Graded Exposure for Anxiety Disorders
population: adults and adolescents with anxiety disorders
clinical_goal: Reduce avoidance and anxiety through systematic, graduated exposure to feared stimuli
content: Graded exposure is the gold-standard behavioural intervention for anxiety disorders including specific phobias, social anxiety disorder, panic disorder with agoraphobia, OCD, and PTSD. The procedure involves: (1) psychoeducation about the anxiety maintenance cycle (the role of avoidance in preserving fear); (2) constructing an exposure hierarchy—a list of 8–12 feared situations ranked from least (SUDS 20–30) to most distressing (SUDS 80–100) using Subjective Units of Distress Scale; (3) conducting exposures starting from the bottom of the hierarchy, remaining in the situation until anxiety reduces by at least 50% or until the client learns that feared consequences do not occur (inhibitory learning model); (4) progressing up the hierarchy session by session. Modern inhibitory learning approaches (Craske et al.) emphasise violating expectancies rather than habituation—exposures should be designed to maximally disconfirm feared predictions. Between-session exposures are assigned as homework.
short_summary: Builds a SUDS-rated fear hierarchy and systematically exposes clients to feared situations to break the avoidance-anxiety cycle.
source_name: Craske & Barlow — Mastery of Your Anxiety and Panic
safety_notes: Must be collaboratively planned; never force or surprise the client. Ensure baseline safety before exposure to trauma cues.
contraindications: Active psychosis, severe self-harm risk, or inability to tolerate distress may require stabilisation before exposure work.

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
clinical_goal: Reduce physiological arousal and teach body-awareness as a coping skill for anxiety and stress
content: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR), developed by Edmund Jacobson, involves systematically tensing and releasing major muscle groups to produce deep physical relaxation and reduce autonomic arousal. Standard protocol covers 16 muscle groups (fists, biceps, triceps, shoulders, neck, forehead, eyes, mouth, jaw, chest, abdomen, lower back, thighs, calves, feet) in a supine or seated position. Each group is tensed for 5–7 seconds then released for 20–30 seconds, with attention to the contrast between tension and relaxation. An abbreviated 4-group version (arms, face/neck, chest/abdomen, legs) can be taught once the full version is mastered. Regular daily practice (15–20 minutes) is needed to build the relaxation response as a conditioned skill. PMR is used adjunctively within CBT for GAD, panic, PTSD, insomnia, chronic pain, and somatic symptom disorders.
short_summary: Teaches systematic tension–release of 16 muscle groups to reduce physiological arousal; an evidence-based coping skill for anxiety and insomnia.
safety_notes: Relaxation-induced anxiety can occur in some clients; introduce slowly and monitor. Avoid tensing injured muscle groups.
contraindications: Contraindicated in clients with musculoskeletal injuries to affected areas. Use caution with recent cardiac events.

title: Problem-Solving Therapy
clinical_goal: Improve effective coping with life stressors by building systematic problem-solving skills
content: Problem-solving therapy (PST) is a CBT-derived intervention that addresses the maladaptive problem orientation and ineffective coping styles that contribute to depression, anxiety, and stress. The intervention follows D'Zurilla and Nezu's model with five steps: (1) Problem orientation—help the client adopt a positive, problem-solving set (viewing problems as normal, solvable challenges rather than threats); (2) Problem definition and formulation—clarify the nature of the problem, separate facts from assumptions, and define a concrete, achievable goal; (3) Generation of alternatives—brainstorm as many solutions as possible without evaluating them (deferred judgment); (4) Decision making—evaluate each option using a cost–benefit analysis (short-term vs long-term consequences, personal vs social impact) and choose the best solution or combination; (5) Solution implementation and verification—implement the chosen solution, evaluate outcome, and troubleshoot if needed. PST is effective for depression, particularly in primary-care settings, and improves self-efficacy.
short_summary: Teaches a five-step model (orientation → definition → brainstorm → decide → verify) to resolve stressors effectively.
source_name: D'Zurilla & Nezu — Problem-Solving Therapy: A Positive Approach to Clinical Intervention
contraindications: May be less effective when presented problems are outside the client's control; ensure focus on controllable aspects.

title: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) Foundations
population: adults with recurrent depression or anxiety
clinical_goal: Develop decentred awareness of thoughts and feelings to prevent depressive relapse and reduce emotional reactivity
content: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT), developed by Segal, Williams and Teasdale, integrates mindfulness meditation practices with CBT to prevent depressive relapse. The programme is structured over 8 weekly group sessions. Core formal practices include: the body scan (45-minute systematic attention to body sensations), mindful movement (gentle yoga), sitting meditation (breath awareness, then open monitoring), and the 3-minute breathing space (a brief portable practice used between sessions). Informal practices involve bringing mindful awareness to daily activities (eating, walking, showering). The key psychological mechanism is decentring: clients learn to observe thoughts and feelings as passing mental events ('I notice I'm having the thought that I'm worthless') rather than facts, reducing the automatic ruminative thinking that triggers depressive episodes. MBCT reduces risk of depressive relapse by approximately 44% in clients with three or more previous episodes.
short_summary: An 8-week group programme combining mindfulness meditation with CBT techniques to prevent depressive relapse through decentred awareness.
source_name: Segal, Williams & Teasdale — Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy for Depression
safety_notes: Intensive mindfulness can temporarily increase distress for trauma survivors; screen for trauma history and offer individual support.
contraindications: Active psychosis or manic episode; severe untreated PTSD where body scan may trigger dissociation.

title: CBT for Insomnia (CBT-I) — Sleep Restriction and Stimulus Control
clinical_goal: Consolidate sleep, reduce time in bed awake, and eliminate conditioned arousal associated with the bed
content: CBT for Insomnia (CBT-I) is the first-line treatment for chronic insomnia, recommended over sleep medication in clinical guidelines (NICE 2022, AAP). The two core behavioural components are: (1) Sleep restriction therapy—initially limit time in bed (TIB) to the client's actual sleep time (minimum 5 hours), creating mild sleep pressure and consolidating fragmented sleep. Each week, if sleep efficiency (SE = time asleep / TIB × 100) exceeds 85%, TIB increases by 15–20 minutes. TIB decreases if SE drops below 80%. (2) Stimulus control—the bed should be reserved for sleep and sex only; if unable to sleep after approximately 20 minutes, get up, go to another room, engage in quiet non-stimulating activity, and return only when sleepy. Both interventions target the two key maintenance factors of insomnia: conditioned arousal (bed → wakefulness association) and excess time in bed. Psychoeducation about sleep drive (Process S) and the circadian system supports adherence.
short_summary: Combines sleep restriction (limiting TIB to actual sleep) and stimulus control (bed for sleep only) to eliminate conditioned arousal and consolidate sleep.
source_name: Morin & Espie — Insomnia: A Clinical Guide to Assessment and Treatment
safety_notes: Sleep restriction may worsen daytime sleepiness initially; advise against driving/operating machinery until adjusted. Not a substitute for sleep apnoea evaluation.
contraindications: Active bipolar disorder (sleep deprivation can trigger mania), seizure disorders, shift work requiring variable sleep schedules.

title: Interoceptive Exposure for Panic Disorder
clinical_goal: Reduce fear of physical sensations by repeatedly inducing and tolerating panic-like bodily sensations
content: Interoceptive exposure (IE) targets the core feature of panic disorder: catastrophic misinterpretation of benign bodily sensations (e.g., heart pounding → 'I'm having a heart attack'). IE involves systematically inducing feared physical sensations in a controlled setting to disconfirm catastrophic predictions. Common induction exercises include: spinning in a chair (dizziness), breathing through a coffee stirrer straw (breathlessness), running on the spot (rapid heartbeat, sweating), staring at a fixed point (derealization), and tensing the body (muscle tension). Each exercise is rated for the similarity to natural panic sensations and for distress level. The client then remains with the sensations without escape or safety behaviours until anxiety subsides, learning that the sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous. IE is delivered after comprehensive psychoeducation on the panic cycle and hyperventilation physiology. Combined with situational exposure (agoraphobia hierarchy), it produces remission rates of 70–90% at 2-year follow-up.
short_summary: Systematically induces panic-like body sensations (spinning, straw breathing) to disconfirm catastrophic misinterpretations and break the fear-of-fear cycle.
source_name: Barlow & Craske — Mastery of Your Anxiety and Panic (MAP)
safety_notes: Always conduct a medical screen first; rule out cardiac, vestibular, and respiratory conditions before inducing sensations.
contraindications: Cardiovascular conditions, severe COPD, vestibular disorders, epilepsy, or recent head trauma.

title: Social Skills Training in CBT
population: adults and adolescents with social anxiety or social difficulties
clinical_goal: Build assertiveness, conversational, and social interaction skills to reduce social anxiety and improve interpersonal functioning
content: Social skills training (SST) is a structured behavioural intervention that targets skills deficits and performance anxiety in social situations. It is commonly delivered within CBT for social anxiety disorder (SAD) and may also be indicated for depression, ASD, and schizophrenia. Core skill domains addressed include: (1) Non-verbal communication—eye contact, facial expression, posture, proximity, and gesturing; (2) Verbal communication—conversation initiation, active listening, turn-taking, appropriate disclosure, and topic maintenance; (3) Assertiveness—expressing needs and opinions, making requests, declining requests, giving and receiving criticism; (4) Interview and presentation skills for specific populations. The training sequence follows instruction → modelling (therapist or video) → rehearsal (role-play in session) → feedback → in vivo homework. Common pitfalls are addressed: post-event processing ('I must have sounded stupid'), the safety behaviours that maintain anxiety (looking away, keeping responses brief), and the self-focused attention that impairs performance. SST is often combined with cognitive restructuring and situational exposure for optimal outcomes.
short_summary: Builds non-verbal, verbal, and assertiveness skills via instruction, modelling, role-play, and homework, combined with cognitive restructuring for social anxiety.
source_name: Hope, Heimberg & Turk — Managing Social Anxiety
safety_notes: Role-play can feel exposing; establish a safe therapeutic relationship first and obtain explicit consent for each exercise.
contraindications: Active psychosis may impair ability to engage in role-play and feedback; stabilise before SST.
